event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins in a dimly lit, upscale lounge with a tense poker game involving four men in suits around a black table laden with chips, cards, and whiskey glasses, where one player declares "I'm all in" amidst sarcastic remarks. The Man in pinstripe suit, focused and intense, abruptly leaves the game upon receiving a call, stating, "You can pay me later, I gotta go." The scene then shifts to a modern office overlooking a nighttime cityscape, where Gerald, a bald man in a dark suit, confronts a woman in a dark blouse by the window, questioning why he's being "screwed" despite paying millions and asking if it's "bed time." The woman introduces "Harvey Specter" as "our best closer," prompting Gerald to question his absence. Harvey explains he specializes in trouble and that the deal wasn't in jeopardy when he left at 7 p.m., seeking to understand the interim developments. Gerald complains about escalating offers being rejected, calling it "last minute bad faith," while Harvey reveals "Cooper won't be staying on as honorary Vice President," which Gerald confirms. The interaction is observed through a glass partition, highlighting a professional yet charged atmosphere. Later, in the same office, Gerald, now in a pinstripe suit, confronts the Man in pinstripe suit (identified as Harvey), accusing him of "dealing in bad faith" regarding an "honorary position" and stating, "he's not getting that title." Gerald sharply agrees with the idea of stripping "Mr. Cooper" of his dignity, but Harvey firmly rejects this, emphasizing his firm's integrity and respect for Mr. Cooper. Gerald then aggressively threatens Harvey, "take your pansy attitude back in there, make him sign my deal. Or I'll pay someone else your money to do it for me." Harvey dismisses the threat, stating that no one will touch the deal after Gerald's bad faith and explaining their agreement. The intense exchange, captured with close-ups, sees Harvey eventually sitting in a blue armchair while Gerald stands, observed by a woman in a dark blazer. Subsequently, Harvey, holding a white document, asserts that "Cooper signed the deal which gave you everything you wanted. Our fee was due and payable, which is why it said on 30. I received confirmation of a wire transfer from escrow, indicating payment in full." He then aggressively tells Gerald, "your balls are in my fist. Now, I apologize if that image is too pansy for you, but I'm comfortable enough with my manhood to put it out there," demanding Gerald "get your ass in there and close the goddamn deal." Gerald challenges him, asking, "You gonna let him talk to me like this?" The woman in the dark blazer calmly interjects, "Harvey speaks for the firm." Gerald then walks away through a glass partition marked 'PEARSON HARDMAN'. The woman approaches Harvey, handing him a document and stating, "We got paid before Gerald signed the deal." Gerald's dismissive voice is then heard questioning the memo's content about a fire drill and the woman's role as blue team captain.

The scene transitions to a restroom, identified by a 'MEN'S ROOM' sign, where the student removes his cap and jacket, revealing a maroon t-shirt, and dries his hands with a paper towel dispenser, his reflection visible in a mirror. The student, now wearing a dark jacket over his maroon shirt, listens intently, his expression shifting from confusion to concern as the man in the vest holds up a small, clear object and asks, "What did you get?" The student defensively replies, "I said I was gonna get you a 158," to which the man retorts, "I told you I wanted a 175." The student explains, "I told you only one out of a hundred people can score that," and the man warns, "You're a B-minus student, you got a thousand on your SATs. If I get you a 175, they'll know you cheated." The student sarcastically asks, "So only a genius loser can get a 175?" and the man confidently asserts, "Actually, no, I would get a 180."

event_blocks.summary: A young man in a dark jacket and glasses, asking "I can have my money, please?", hands over cash in a dimly lit hallway, only for the man in the light blue checkered shirt to count it and state "This is only half." The young man retorts, "Then why don't you go call the police?" as a car drives away and an upbeat, jazzy song about being a "fraud" begins. The scene shifts to a nighttime city street where a man in a dark suit and striped tie walks purposefully into a dimly lit restaurant, scanning the room until his eyes meet a woman's. She asks, "Nervous?" and he replies, "I'd be lying if I said no," to which she responds, "Well, I wouldn't want you to lie." He then gravely states, "She knows. If she can't, she dies." The setting changes to a modern office with glass partitions and a 'PEARSON HARDMAN' sign, where the man in the suit walks confidently past a reception area. Another man in a dark suit observes him, asking, "What's holding up my swindling merger?" to which the first man incredulously replies, "Are you kidding me?" Two men in dark suits, one in gray with a red tie and the other in black with a patterned tie, walk through a well-lit office hallway discussing a woman named Jessica who is summoning them for dinner. The man in the black suit asserts, "We've been over this, okay? If she knew, you'd know," while the man in the gray suit defensively states, "I do know. The woman barely knows my name and she just summoned me for dinner." The man in the gray suit lists names like "Watson, Clyman, Gallo, Goldberg, Inspector," explaining that "Every one of us was taken to dinner by Jessica as a first year, and now she's taking you, which means..." The man in the black suit interrupts, "This is a good thing," but the man in the gray suit panics, asking, "What do I wear? What what do I say? What do I wear?" The man in the gray suit demands to know "what the hell's holding up my Swinton merger," and the man in the black suit dismisses it as "just a nuisance suit that needs to be cleaned up before we can clear escrow." The man in the gray suit accuses him of not being told sooner and sarcastically references "Goodfellas" out of fear of being fired, shouting, "Caren! Where's the money, Caren!" The man in the black suit tells him to "Go back to your cubicle" and walks away, leaving the man in the gray suit hopeful about getting an office from the dinner. The man in the black suit gestures back toward him near a 'PEARSON HARDMAN' glass partition.

event_blocks.summary: In a dimly lit, upscale restaurant with warm ambient lighting and wooden paneling, soft, melancholic piano music plays as a man in a dark suit and tie asks a woman in a dark blouse, "About what?" She playfully responds, "Hope you brought your checkbook. Dinner's on you," eliciting a slight chuckle from him as he sips his glass. He then asks, "Wait, what was Harvey right about?" as the piano music intensifies. The scene transitions to a modern office environment with glass partitions, where the woman, now in a gray wrap dress and holding a document, approaches a man in a dark suit and striped tie seated at a desk. He stands, and they exchange a tense, wordless glance. The man asks, "How was dinner?" to which she replies, "Free caviar. What's not to love?" The piano music takes on a more dramatic, investigative tone as the man confronts her, stating, "If you want to know something about me, you should have just asked me." She counters, "Why can't I just be getting to know Mr. Ross the way I got to know you?" He responds with a focused, slightly wary demeanor, "Because if you're just trying to get to know him, you would have told me about the dinner. Something's going on, and I want to know what it is." The woman, with a serious and determined expression, reveals, "An allegation was made that Mike Ross never went to Harvard or any other law school for that matter. So I had him checked out." She concludes, "Squeaky clean. Graduated top of his class," as the office's large window overlooks the city. Later, in a modern, dimly lit upscale apartment overlooking a city skyline at night, the woman explains to the man, "So I took him to dinner. I wanted to see what you saw in him that makes him so special," to which he calmly replies, "And?" She continues, "I get it. He's a total package. Maybe even the best of both of us." He expresses concern, "I hope this doesn't mean you're gonna put me in charge of recruiting." She reassures him, "Oh, not to worry, you are off the hook," presenting a document with the sound of rustling paper. Addressing him as "Harvey," she reveals, "He might have checked out clean. But I know that guy... Because there may be a record of him graduating from Harvard Law, but there's no record of him graduating from any college, anywhere on the face of the earth." On-screen text identifies the episode as 'THE ARRANGEMENT' Season 3, Episode 1, over a shot of a woman lying on a bed in a modest apartment. Her voice becomes commanding as she states, "So I'm going to give you the benefit of the doubt. That you didn't know. Because I don't want to do what I would do if you did. And what you're going to do is fire that goddamn kid." Dramatic, building music with a male vocalist singing, "I hope you make it alright," concludes the segment.

event_blocks.summary: In a modern, dimly lit apartment overlooking a city skyline at night, accompanied by an upbeat, tense male vocalist track, Jessica, wearing a black leather jacket and white pencil skirt, sips from a glass at a kitchen island. Harvey, in a white dress shirt and dark tie, enters, removes his suit jacket, and confidently sits in a black leather armchair, legs crossed. Jessica approaches Harvey, initiating a tense conversation by confronting him, "I don't recall authorizing you to negotiate behind Darren Williams' back," her expression shifting from calm to assertive. Harvey dismisses her concern about "Darren Williams' back" and confidently states, "I'm standing right behind it." Jessica challenges him, asking why a signed contract is in her hand and "How much did you ask for?" Harvey bluntly replies, "Triple." Jessica, incredulous, asks if he's "out of [his] mind," to which Harvey calmly asserts, "We have leverage." When pressed, he evasively says, "It's complicated," before admitting, "I bluffed him." Jessica dismisses this as not leverage, but Harvey insists it is "if he believes it." She highlights the high stakes, warning that if it fails, "he fires us and takes ten other players with him," to which Harvey confidently retorts, "Then it better not fail." Jessica, exasperated, reminds him, "You asked the man for triple," but Harvey justifies his actions and bluffing strategy. Jessica, frustrated, tells him not to bluff, but Harvey defiantly states, "I'm afraid that's what I do." Growing suspicious, Jessica accuses him, "I think you wanted it to fail. I think you're stuck with a merger you don't want and a non-compete you can't stand, and you're trying to get me to fire you," as Harvey begins to respond, "I could..." The warm, focused lighting highlights their exchange against the dark, urban backdrop and luxurious, minimalist decor.

event_blocks.summary: In a modern apartment, a man in a dark suit and striped tie, seated in a black leather armchair, tells Jessica, "that non-compete wide open" and "Which is why I'm staying," asserting, "I've always operated this way. It's what you hired me for. It's what you pay me for, and it used to be what you valued me for." Jessica, in a dark blazer with a gold necklace, responds, "I'm not going to fire you." The man stands, holding a smartphone, and mentions, "Neither is Darren Williams. Nets just caved," before asking dismissively, "unless you're looking to cook me breakfast tomorrow? I'd say we're done." An upbeat, melancholic rock song begins as the scene shifts to a modern apartment interior where Jessica, now in a white blouse and light shorts, walks toward a door. A man in a black jacket and white t-shirt enters, looking surprised, and surveys the dimly lit living room with a round table, yellow tulips, a gray sofa, and patterned curtains. Jessica, arms crossed and stern, states, "I told you I needed time." The man, his expression shifting to contemplation, urgently replies, "I know, but it's been two days and I can't take it anymore. I had to see you." After a pause, Jessica warns, "I don't want any comments about how messy the place is," as a door closes. The man, moving further into the room, sarcastically asks, "Okay. And what would clean look like?" Jessica cuts him off, "Don't even think about it," before he can finish, "I can't not think..." The atmosphere remains tense. Later, in the same dimly lit apartment, Rachel, in a white wrap dress, stands with her arms crossed, engaging in a tense conversation with Mike, who has just entered wearing a black jacket over a white t-shirt. Mike, appearing surprised and defensive, explains, "It's all I've been able to think about for two days." Rachel confronts him, "Mike, why are you here? Because that's not gonna happen," to which he questions, "Now or ever?" She accuses him, "You lied to me about everything." Mike explains his need for time "to process this bomb that you dropped on me," adding that he "had a nightmare," and clarifies, "You exposed me to the entire firm," which Rachel denies. Mike then reveals his nightmare: "if you didn't want to be with me." Rachel, her expression softening, asks, "Do you really mean that?" Mike earnestly replies, "I meant it the first time I ever saw you." As melancholic music swells, Mike pleads, "I know that I'm not who you want me to be, but you know what happened in that room, you know what we both felt. I don't want to lose that." The camera alternates between close-ups of their faces, capturing Rachel's skepticism and Mike's earnestness, while the background reveals framed abstract art, a tufted bed, and a bookshelf, the shadows enhancing the emotional weight of their intimate yet charged interaction.

In a modern, glass-walled office with expansive city views, a Man in a dark suit and blue tie initiates a firm, professional handshake with an Older man, bald with a white beard and wearing a dark suit with a patterned pink tie, signaling the conclusion of an initial agreement. The Man in dark suit, with a confident and strategic voice, responds, "Good," explaining that a "yes is always so much better when it starts as a no." As the Older man sits at a sleek black conference table, reviewing documents, he acknowledges the younger man's confidence but asserts the worth of his distribution centers. event_blocks.summary: In a modern high-rise office, an Older man with a white beard and patterned pink tie, seated at a glass conference table, authoritatively questions a Man in a dark suit, identified as Walter, about his intentions with distribution centers, to which Walter confidently declares he will reveal his plans after acquiring them. Walter, with a serious expression, then stands and exits the room carrying a black briefcase. The scene shifts to a relaxed office lounge where upbeat, jazzy instrumental music plays. Jessica sharply told Louis not to call Harvey, to which Harvey agreed to move forward. The situation escalated with the unexpected entrance of a man in a black hoodie carrying a brown paper bag, apologizing for interrupting and stating he had an order for Jessica Pearson. Harvey expressed disbelief that an emergency meeting was preceded by a Chinese food order. Louis, taking the bag, aggressively confronted the man, who then revealed he was not delivering food but serving a legal document. Jessica intervened, urging Louis to calm down, noting the food order was a mistake, but Louis insisted the order was for Jessica Pearson, confirming her identity. The man in the hoodie then revealed his true purpose: to serve Jessica Pearson with the document, not an egg roll.

raw_segments: The scene unfolds in a dimly lit, modern office or lounge space with large windows overlooking a city skyline at night, creating a sophisticated and tense atmosphere. A woman, identified as Jessica Pearson, in a black dress with sheer long sleeves and a delicate silver pendant necklace, sits on a gray sofa, holding a glass of whiskey, her expression serious and observant. She challenges, "With what lawyers? What resources and what money? Because I don't know if you've noticed but this firm has been gutted because of your recommendation." Two men in dark suits enter, their movements deliberate and purposeful. Louis retorts, "No, Jessica, it was gutted because of mine." Harvey then addresses Jessica, stating, "You're right, Louis, I did. But I didn't come down here to play pin the blame on the Harvey. I came down here to figure out what the hell we're going to do." Jessica, with a sharp tone, tells Louis, "Damn it, Louis, I told you not to call him." Harvey adds, "Then let's get to it." A third man, wearing a black hoodie with white Chinese characters and carrying a brown paper bag, enters through a glass door, his presence unexpected. He apologizes, "Oh, I'm sorry to interrupt but there was no one at reception and I have your order here." Harvey, incredulous, exclaims, "You gotta be kidding me. You call me down here for an emergency and then you take the time to order Chinese food?" Louis, taking the bag, aggressively warns, "I didn't order anything, asshole, so crouch your tiger, hide your dragon or I'm going to wang chung your ass out of here." Jessica interjects, "Louis, calm down. Clearly, there's some mistake, no one at this firm has ordered any food." Louis insists, "Well, I'm pretty sure this is the right place because the name on your door is Jessica Pearson and this order is for a Jessica Pearson." Jessica confirms, "Yeah, this is Jessica Pearson." Louis then asks, "What?" as the man in the hoodie reveals his true purpose, stating, "Do you know I'm not really here to bring you an egg roll, asshole. I'm here to serve you with this." The woman watches the exchange closely, her posture relaxed but her gaze sharp, suggesting she is assessing the dynamics, her calm demeanor contrasting with the men’s intensity, suggesting she holds a position of power or influence. event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins in a dimly lit, luxurious apartment at night, where a woman in a black dress with sheer long sleeves sits pensively on a gray sofa, holding a white document and gazing at a city skyline as a male voice states, "It's a class action," followed by her declaration, "We're being sued for every case Mike Ross has ever touched," accompanied by ominous, suspenseful background music. The scene shifts to a bright, sunlit residential street during the day, where a black convertible with a yellow smiley-face sticker is parked. A man in a black suit and patterned tie exits the car, the sound of the door closing, and approaches a blonde woman in a dark coat over a white sweater holding a coffee cup, who greets him, "Harvey. What are you doing here?" As they walk, Harvey explains he was "driving this baby around," and when she questions if it's meant to impress her, he confidently replies, "I hope so. Sure as hell impressed me." She professionally states, "Well, it's nice to see you, but if you wanted an appointment, you should have called." Harvey clarifies, "I'm not here for an appointment. I'm here to ask you out," prompting her surprised, "You're serious." He elaborates on his personal and professional growth, "I'm doing a lot of thinking lately... I'm not the same man that I was. I made peace with my family, I got my old associate back, and as of this morning, I'm the head of my firm. And when I thought of sharing that with someone... I thought of you." The woman reveals her profession, countering, "Well, it doesn't matter because it's completely unethical for a doctor to engage in a relationship with her patient," to which Harvey begins to argue, "Not if enough time has passed. And according to the APA, as of three weeks..." The episode is identified on-screen as 'SKIN IN THE GAME' from Season 7, Episode 1, as their interaction continues in the upscale neighborhood.

event_blocks.summary: On a sunlit residential street, Nathan, a man in a black suit and patterned tie, walks with a woman in a dark coat over a white sweater, discussing a meeting and proposing "Thursday at 8 or Saturday at 9?" The woman agrees to "Thursday" for "one dinner," stipulating that if their chemistry doesn't align, they'll "part as friends, no hard feelings," to which Nathan agrees, "If it doesn't work out, no harm, no foul." As they approach a black convertible with a yellow smiley-face sticker, Nathan opens the driver's door, and they both get in. The car's engine starts and revs loudly, prompting Nathan to ask if she'd prefer Ray to drive, but she responds, "I like the car," shouting it over the engine's increased revs. The car drives away down the tree-lined street, accompanied by upbeat rock music that fades out. The scene shifts to a dimly lit, modern office where Nathan, now in a gray suit, enters holding papers and approaches a seated Man in gray suit who greets him, "Good. You're here early," and assigns him to "take point on a consumer fraud hearing for Sandra." Nathan interrupts, "Nathan, I need to talk to you," before the seated man can respond. Later, in the same office, a man in a dark suit and patterned tie stands before Nathan, who is seated at a desk, initiating a tense conversation about past events. Nathan interjects, "I'm not," when the standing man asks if he's still upset. The man in the dark suit then hands Nathan a document, revealing a check for $500,000, explaining he's returning to Pearson Specter Litt and insisted on this settlement to avoid leaving Nathan "high and dry." Nathan questions if Harvey Specter is "buying him back," but the man in the dark suit asserts he insisted on the pro bono cases moving forward, which Nathan suggests is to "make himself feel better." The man in the dark suit clarifies, "No, Nathan. I did that because I care about doing this kind of work," and proposes partnering on cases, but Nathan firmly asks, "What if I said I'm not okay with any of this?" The man in the dark suit appeals to Nathan's past support, reminding him, "I know that you were the one that gave me another chance to be a lawyer when no one else would, but don't punish..."

The woman steps forward, speaking with a mix of urgency and composure, responding, "Well, to be fair, you didn't see what was right in front of your face for 12 years." Harvey concedes, "Fair enough. But it does beg the question." The woman then suggests, "Let's not tell Louis or anybody else?" to which Harvey asks, "Why not?" She explains, "Because Louis might be clueless, but he's right. There's gonna be a lot of blowback from the hearing and I'd rather share our news when the people we care about are in the right frame of mind to hear it." Harvey then brings up a past conversation, "About that news. Before Louis got here, you asked what had changed." The woman, addressing Harvey directly, states, "Harvey, I want to know everything and when I do, I want to be able to hang on every last word, but now's not the time." The camera alternates between close-ups of their faces and wider shots, emphasizing the emotional weight of this high-stakes personal or professional conflict, all under soft, natural lighting.
